title: Compound Interest: Jake has 698.45 in an account which pays 0.29 compounded...
summary: Calculate the effective annual rate or convert between APR and EAR under different compounding assumptions.
prompt: Jake has $698.45 in an account which pays 29% compounded annually. How much more additional interest can he earn over four years if he moved his money to an account which pays 30% compounded annually?
solution: To determine how much additional interest Jake can earn by moving his money from an account paying 29% compounded annually to one paying 30% compounded annually over four years, we can follow these steps: 1. **Calculate the future value (FV) of the current account (29% compounded annually):** - Present Value (PV) = $698.45 - Annual interest rate (r) = 29% or 0.29 - Number of years (t) = 4 The formula for future value with annual compounding is: \[ FV = PV \times (1 + r)^t \] Applying the values: \[ FV_{29} = 698.45 \times (1 + 0.29)^4 \] \[ FV_{29} = 698.45 \times (1.29)^4 \] \[ FV_{29} = 698.45 \times 2.207 \] \[ FV_{29} \approx 1540.92 \] 2. **Calculate the future value (FV) of the new account (30% compounded annually):** - Present Value (PV) = $698.45 - Annual interest rate (r) = 30% or 0.30 - Number of years (t) = 4 Applying the values: \[ FV_{30} = 698.45 \times (1 + 0.30)^4 \] \[ FV_{30} = 698.45 \times (1.30)^4 \] \[ FV_{30} = 698.45 \times 2.8561 \] \[ FV_{30} \approx 1993.13 \] 3. **Calculate the additional interest earned by moving the money:** \[ \text{Additional Interest} = FV_{30} - FV_{29} \] \[ \text{Additional Interest} = 1993.13 - 1540.92 \] \[ \text{Additional Interest} \approx 452.21 \] Therefore, Jake can earn approximately $452.21 more in additional interest over four years by moving his money to an account that pays 30% compounded annually instead of 29%.
